draft.introduction: The Plague Prayers of Mursili II (c. 1320 BCE) are Hittite royal prayers seeking to understand and end a devastating plague. The king confesses the sins of his father and the nation, making this one of the earliest examples of communal penitential prayer.

translation.text: O Storm God of Hatti, my lord, and you gods my lords: Mursili, your servant, has sent me, saying: ‘Go, speak to the Storm God thus: What is this that you have done? You have let a plague into the land. The land of Hatti is dying. No one prepares offering bread and libation for you any more. We have searched the oracles, and the sin of my father has been found.’

translator_notes.note: Mursili’s willingness to confess his father’s treaty violations as the cause of plague reflects a theology of corporate responsibility. His pragmatic argument—if worshipers all die, no one will make offerings—introduces a utilitarian appeal to divine self-interest that has no parallel in biblical prayer.
